_metadata.description: Proprietary blend recognition database for supplement label mapping
_metadata.schema_notes: This file is for MAPPING only. It identifies and categorizes proprietary blend names found on supplement labels. Penalty scoring is handled entirely by the scorer (B5 engine) based on disclosure_level (full/partial/none) — NOT by this file. Each entry has: id, standard_name, blend_terms (label text patterns), risk_factors (safety concerns for user-facing notes), notes (user-facing explanation shown in product detail view).

risk_factors: Hidden caffeine doses may exceed safe daily limits (400mg/day FDA guideline)
Undisclosed stimulant combinations can cause synergistic cardiovascular stress
Yohimbine, synephrine, or DMAA may be present without labeling
Stimulant stacking increases risk of arrhythmia and hypertension
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Stimulant blends combine caffeine with other stimulants like synephrine, yohimbine, or theobromine. Without individual dosage disclosure, total stimulant load cannot be assessed. FDA has issued multiple warnings about undisclosed stimulants in pre-workout and energy supplements.

risk_factors: Hidden prohormones or SARMs found in FDA enforcement actions (2017-2024)
Undeclared anabolic steroids detected in third-party testing
Liver toxicity risk from undisclosed methylated compounds
Hormone disruption from unknown compound ratios
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Testosterone and hormone support blends carry the highest contamination risk of any supplement category. FDA and DOD testing consistently finds undeclared anabolic agents, SARMs, and prohormones. Products with undisclosed formulas in this category warrant particular caution.

risk_factors: Sibutramine (banned) is the most common adulterant in weight loss supplements
Phenolphthalein and other laxatives found undeclared in FDA testing
Hidden stimulants (DMAA, BMPEA) pose cardiovascular risk
Dangerous drug combinations undetectable without full disclosure
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Weight loss supplements have the highest rate of FDA enforcement actions. A 2018 JAMA study found 776 adulterated weight loss products over a decade, with sibutramine, phenolphthalein, and fluoxetine as top adulterants. Full ingredient disclosure is essential for safety in this category.

risk_factors: Pharmaceutical nootropics (modafinil, piracetam) found undeclared
Unknown racetam-type compounds at uncontrolled doses
Serotonergic ingredients can interact with antidepressants
Neurological effects from undisclosed compound combinations
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Cognitive enhancement blends increasingly contain pharmaceutical-grade nootropics without disclosure. FDA has identified products containing undeclared prescription drugs including modafinil analogs. Consumers on psychiatric medications face interaction risks when blend contents are hidden.

risk_factors: Hidden vasodilators may cause hypotension in sensitive individuals
Excessive nitrate dosing risk without individual amounts
Sodium/fluid balance disruption from undisclosed electrolyte content
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Pump and nitric oxide blends combine L-citrulline, L-arginine, beetroot extract, and other vasodilators. Without individual dosing, users on blood pressure medications or PDE5 inhibitors cannot assess interaction risk. Excessive vasodilation can cause dangerous hypotension.

risk_factors: Adaptogen potency varies 10-100x between sources and extracts
Unknown ratios prevent assessment of herb-drug interactions
Ashwagandha, rhodiola, and holy basil can affect thyroid function
Sedative herbs in undisclosed amounts may impair alertness
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Adaptogen blends combine herbs like ashwagandha, rhodiola, and ginseng that modulate the HPA axis. Without individual doses, consumers cannot assess thyroid interactions (ashwagandha), MAO inhibition potential (rhodiola), or additive sedation. Clinical doses vary dramatically by extract standardization.

blend_terms: Ear, Nose & Throat Support Herbal Blend
orchard fruits & garden veggies powder blend
Orchard Fruits & Garden Veggies Powder Blend
mushroom mycelial & primordia biomass defense blend
risk_factors: Individual ingredient amounts unknown — therapeutic doses unlikely
Herbal adulterant risk in multi-herb blends (Botanical Adulterants Prevention Program)
Potential heavy metal accumulation from mixed botanical sources
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Superfood and herbal blends typically contain many ingredients in amounts too small for therapeutic effect. Without individual doses, it is impossible to determine whether any component reaches a clinically meaningful level. Multi-herb blends also carry higher contamination risk per the Botanical Adulterants Prevention Program.

risk_factors: Individual strain CFU counts unknown — efficacy is strain-specific and dose-dependent
Total CFU may be dominated by one cheap filler strain
Strain ratios affect competitive exclusion and colonization success
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Probiotic efficacy is strain-specific and dose-dependent (e.g., L. rhamnosus GG requires ≥10B CFU). When blends disclose only total CFU without per-strain breakdown, it is impossible to verify whether any strain reaches its clinically studied dose. Some blends use high-count filler strains to inflate total CFU.

risk_factors: Enzyme activity levels (FCC/USP units) not disclosed per enzyme
Unknown ratios of protease, lipase, amylase prevent dosing assessment
Enteric coating claims unverifiable without composition data
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Digestive enzyme blends should disclose individual enzyme activity levels in standardized units (FCC or USP). Without per-enzyme activity data, it is impossible to assess whether the blend provides clinically meaningful digestive support. Enzyme activity varies dramatically by source and processing.

risk_factors: Unknown collagen peptide dosages — clinical doses are 2.5-15g/day
Hidden hormones or growth factors in anti-aging formulas
Biotin mega-doses can interfere with lab test results
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Beauty blends combine collagen peptides, biotin, hyaluronic acid, and botanical extracts. Clinical evidence requires specific doses (e.g., 2.5g Verisol collagen peptides for skin elasticity). Without individual amounts, efficacy cannot be assessed and potential lab test interference from high-dose biotin goes undetected.

risk_factors: Sodium/potassium ratios unknown — critical for cardiac and renal patients
Hidden stimulants in recovery products
Electrolyte imbalance risk without individual mineral amounts
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Electrolyte blends require individual mineral disclosure (sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium) for safe use. Patients on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics need exact potassium content. WHO oral rehydration standards specify precise electrolyte ratios that cannot be verified without disclosure.

risk_factors: Undisclosed excipients may include allergens (soy lecithin, shellac)
Processing aids and binding agents hidden under technology branding
Absorption enhancers (piperine, BioPerine) can affect drug metabolism
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Delivery technology blends wrap excipients, binders, and absorption enhancers under branded names. While generally low-risk, consumers with allergies need full excipient disclosure, and absorption enhancers like piperine can increase drug bioavailability and affect CYP3A4/P-gp substrates.

risk_factors: Complete lack of ingredient-level transparency
Unknown ratios make efficacy assessment impossible
Ineffective micro-dosing hidden behind blend totals
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: General proprietary blends hide individual ingredient amounts behind a single total weight. This makes it impossible to verify whether any ingredient reaches a clinically effective dose or to assess safety for individuals with specific health conditions or medication interactions.

risk_factors: Protein spiking with cheap amino acids (glycine, taurine) to inflate protein content
Unknown whey/casein/soy ratios in multi-source blends
Amino acid profiles unverifiable without individual amounts
proprietary_blend_concerns.notes: Protein blends may use amino acid spiking (adding cheap free-form amino acids like glycine or taurine) to inflate nitrogen content and apparent protein percentage. Without individual protein source amounts, consumers cannot verify protein quality or detect spiking. Third-party tested products with full disclosure are preferred.
